flashcards.front: Define the Divide and Conquer paradigm
flashcards.back: Divide and Conquer is an algorithm design paradigm that breaks a problem into smaller subproblems, solves them recursively, and combines their solutions. The three steps are: Divide (break problem), Conquer (solve subproblems), and Combine (merge solutions).

flashcards.front: Describe the Merge Sort algorithm steps
flashcards.back: Merge Sort divides the array into two halves, recursively sorts each half, then merges the sorted halves. Base case: when subarray has ≤1 element. The merge procedure compares elements from both halves and builds a sorted output array.

flashcards.front: What are the time and space complexities of Merge Sort?
flashcards.back: Time Complexity: O(n log n) in all cases (worst, average, best). Space Complexity: O(n) auxiliary space for the temporary array during merging. Merge Sort is stable and not in-place due to the merge step requiring extra memory.

flashcards.front: Describe the Quick Sort algorithm with partition step
flashcards.back: Quick Sort selects a pivot element, partitions the array around the pivot (elements < pivot left, > pivot right), then recursively sorts the partitions. Partition function: pivot = arr[high], i = low-1, swap elements < pivot to position i+1, place pivot at correct position.

flashcards.front: List and explain Quick Sort pivot selection strategies
flashcards.back: 1) First element as pivot - simple but poor for sorted input. 2) Last element as pivot - common but vulnerable to sorted data. 3) Random element - reduces worst-case probability. 4) Median-of-three - selects median of first, middle, last elements. 5) Lomuto partition scheme - uses last element, handles duplicates better.

flashcards.front: What are the time and space complexities of Quick Sort?
flashcards.back: Time: O(n log n) average case, O(n²) worst case (when array is already sorted and pivot is first/last element). Space: O(log n) auxiliary for recursion stack in average case, O(n) worst case. Quick Sort is unstable and in-place.

flashcards.front: Compare Merge Sort vs Quick Sort
flashcards.back: Merge Sort: stable, O(n) space, O(n log n) always, better for linked lists and external sorting. Quick Sort: unstable, O(log n) space avg, faster in practice due to cache locality, better for arrays. Quick Sort preferred for arrays; Merge Sort for linked lists and when stability matters.

flashcards.front: Practical applications of Merge Sort and Quick Sort
flashcards.back: Merge Sort: external sorting (large files), counting inversions, merge step in Timsort (Python/Java), stable sorting required. Quick Sort: in-memory sorting of arrays, database sort (quicksort hybrid with median-of-three), C++ std::sort (introsort variant), general-purpose sorting when average performance matters.
